deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this patient.
Let me go through the case step by step.
The patient is a 29-year-old woman presenting with a soft-tissue mass in the head of the pancreas.
She had been doing well until 7 weeks prior when she developed pruritus, nausea, bloating, and watery mustard-colored diarrhea.
She had eaten sushi the night before, which makes me think about possible food poisoning, maybe from a parasite like Giardia or something similar.
She was treated with methylprednisolone, hydroxyzine, and diphenoxylate-atropine, but her symptoms didn't improve.
Then jaundice developed along with right upper quadrant abdominal pain and amber urine.
Her lab work showed elevated ALT, AST, and bilirubin, which points towards liver issues.
The CA 19-9 was 73, which is a marker for pancreatic cancer, especially pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.
Imaging showed dilated biliary and pancreatic ducts, a double-duct sign, and a hypodense mass in the pancreatic head.
The CA 19-9 level was high, which is a strong indicator for pancreatic cancer.
The mass was seen on CT and MRI, and ERCP showed a stricture in the bile duct with a stent placed.
The bilirubin initially decreased but then rose again, and the CA 19-9 also increased, suggesting a possible obstruction or progression.
The patient declined surgery and was treated with steroids, which helped her symptoms.
However, the mass didn't resolve, and she was eventually diagnosed with a pancreatic mass, likely a cystic or solid lesion.
The final diagnosis is probably pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma given the elevated CA 19-9 and imaging findings.
